title: Henrique Santos
text: The Science Behind From Screen to Living Room

title: Henrique Santos
text:
I am a Developmental Psychology researcher at the University of California, Santa Cruz, working within the CRECE Lab. My research explores the cognitive and socio-emotional mechanisms that shape how children understand complex life concepts, such as death and moral reasoning.
My work is driven by the goal of making developmental science accessible and impactful. I am particularly interested in how animation and media can serve as catalysts for parent-child conversations, turning complex biological and social topics into opportunities for connection and learning.
Research Interests:

Cognitive Development & Biological Understanding
Moral Reasoning and Character Perception
Media as a Developmental Tool

title: Henrique Santos
text:

My work is driven by the goal of making developmental science accessible and impactful. I am particularly interested in how animation and media can serve as catalysts for parent-child conversations, turning complex biological and social topics into opportunities for connection and learning.

title: Current Projects
text:
Explore my ongoing research at the intersection of developmental psychology and children’s media.


Animated Bridges: Scaffolding Parent-Child Conversations About Death






Children encounter the concept of death early through media and personal experience. This project investigates how parents can use animated films to scaffold their 4- to 6-year-old children’s understanding of death. By observing families during online interviews and shared viewing of short animated films, we explore how media acts as a tool for navigating complex conversations.
Preliminary Findings: Our current data suggests that most families mentioned death in their conversations after watching an animation. However, these conversations are often brief, with parents assuming an active role and focused primarily on the affective aspects of death. This focus on emotional aspects of death might suggest parents desire to prepare their children with socioemotional skills.

The Cultural Ecology of Death: How Culture Shape Parent-Child Conversations






Expanding on the previous methodology, this project examines how cultural context influences the way parents and children navigate the concept of death. By recruiting participant populations from Brazil and Colombia, we aim to identify cross-cultural variations in scaffolding strategies and conceptual emphasis. The goal is to determine how cultural values and narratives shape the interactions between parents and children during co-viewing.

Heroes, Villains, and Loss: Emotional Attribution and Empathy in Fictional Death






From heroes to “sympathetic villains,” animated characters provide a playground for moral development. This study explores how characters’ morals influence children’s perception of death. Children (ages 4-10) evaluate morally “good,” “bad,” and “mixed” characters (e.g., characters that did good and bad stuff) within animated narratives. We specifically investigate how a character’s moral status informs a child’s emotional attribution and empathy following the character’s death.
Preliminary Findings:

The Architecture of Learning: How Animated Features Shape Early Science Comprehension






Animation is a powerful tool for public health communication. In this project, we analyze how specific features of animated media, such as visual representations (e.g., anthropomorphism), affect how children understand viruses and illness. By testing child comprehension after viewing different educational animations about COVID-19, we aim to identify if the way the information is presented to children (i.e., in a realistic or animistic model) influence children’s learning about COVID-19 and transfering to other viruses.
Preliminary Findings:

Mapping the Silence: A Needs Assessment of Parental Challenges and Strategies in Death Conversations






This project utilizes a large-scale parental survey to assess the current landscape of parent-child conversations regarding death. By documenting families’ past experiences, the specific strategies employed, and the challenges faced, we aim to quantify the perceived need for external support in navigating these discussions.
Research Goals: The data gathered will serve to identify successful naturalistic strategies used by parents and pinpoint common conceptual or emotional roadblocks. These findings will directly inform the development of a research-based guide designed to support parents in managing these challenging conversations and provide them with the tools they have identified as most beneficial for their family dynamics.

title: Current Research
section: I investigate how children evaluate the actions of heroes and villains in media. My recent studies look at children’s reactions to character deaths and how these moments serve as “teachable moments” for moral development.
text: I investigate how children evaluate the actions of heroes and villains in media. My recent studies look at children’s reactions to character deaths and how these moments serve as “teachable moments” for moral development.

title: Media & Animation
text: My work utilizes 2D minimalistic animation to bridge the gap between scientific findings and public health communication. By focusing on clear storytelling and relatable characters, these animations serve as tools for both education and research.

title: Media
text:
I use 2D minimalistic animation to create controlled environments for developmental studies.



Engagement: Children respond naturally to animated storytelling.
Precision: It allows us to isolate specific behaviors or moral dilemmas without the noise of live-action video.

I am a PhD Candidate in Developmental Psychology at the University of California, Santa Cruz, working within the CRECE Lab. My research is driven by a fundamental question: How can animation be a tool for children’s learning and development?
By combining my background in developmental science with storytelling, I investigate how media sparks the parent-child conversations that help children navigate complex concepts.

title: Current Research
text:
Explore my ongoing research at the intersection of developmental psychology, media, and cross-cultural understanding.








Study Overview




This project investigates how children conceptualize the biological and emotional aspects of death. My work focuses on how animated storytelling can serve as a catalyst for parent-child conversations, helping children navigate these complex topics. This research was recently presented at the CDS 2026 conference in Montreal.











Cross-Cultural Comparison




Building on my core research, this international collaboration compares developmental trajectories across diverse cultural contexts, including populations in Brazil and Colombia. We examine how cultural narratives and family dynamics shape the acquisition of biological knowledge regarding the end of life.











Moral Development Study




This study explores how children perceive “villains” and morally ambiguous characters in animated media. We are investigating whether children’s ability to distinguish between moral and conventional transgressions in stories correlates with their social-emotional development and empathy levels.











Educational Media Analysis




A retrospective analysis of educational video content produced during the pandemic. This project evaluates the effectiveness of rapid-response media in communicating health science to families and the role of digital storytelling in mitigating educational disruptions for young learners.

title: For Parents
text:
Welcome! This space is dedicated to translating developmental science into practical insights for parents and creators. Here, we explore how stories and animation can help us navigate complex conversations with children.

Translating ScienceExternal Resources


Summaries of research written for families and media creators.

[Post 1] Why “Villains” Matter for Moral Growth






Does watching “bad guys” help kids be good? We look at how morally ambiguous characters in animated films help children practice distinguishing between social rules and fundamental morals. Read the full post →






[Post 2] Animation: A Tool for Tough Topics






Talking about death doesn’t have to be scary. Learn how high-quality storytelling provides a “safe distance” for children to ask big questions about life and biology. Read the full post →






A curated list of tools and guides from the broader developmental community.

For Conversations

Sesame Workshop: Resources for talking to children about grief and difficult transitions.
Common Sense Media: Age-based ratings and reviews focusing on the educational and moral value of films.



For Media Creators

Center for Scholars & Storytellers: Research-backed tip sheets for authentic representation of childhood in media.
The Fred Rogers Center: Guidelines on using digital media in ways that support healthy relationships.

title: Media & Animation
text:
Featured Work

COVID-19 Explained for Families






Project Overview: Developed during the pandemic, this animation was designed to explain complex health science to children and their parents. It uses simplified visual metaphors to reduce anxiety and provide actionable health information.

title: Media & Animation
text:
Future Projects & Prototypes
I am currently developing a series of short animated films designed to facilitate parent-child conversations about biological concepts and social-emotional growth.

Project Death: A series of shorts exploring the biological cycle of life.
The “Villain” Series: Exploring moral ambiguity through character design.
